sections.heading: What is Room?
sections.content: Room is the official Android Jetpack library for local database persistence, built as an abstraction layer on top of SQLite. While SQLite is powerful, writing raw SQL queries directly in Android code is error-prone, verbose, and produces no compile-time verification of SQL correctness. Room solves this by mapping Kotlin data classes to database tables, validating SQL at compile time, and providing a clean coroutine and Flow-based API for all database operations. Room is the recommended solution for any app that needs to persist structured data locally on the device.

sections.heading: Why Use Room?
items: Compile-time SQL verification — incorrect queries cause build errors, not runtime crashes
No boilerplate — Room generates all the SQLite cursor and ContentValues code automatically
Kotlin coroutine support — suspend functions for one-shot operations, Flow for reactive queries
Flow integration — database changes automatically emit new values through a Flow observable
Type converters — store non-primitive types like Date, List, or custom objects using TypeConverters
Migration support — structured database schema migrations with version tracking
Relationship support — @Relation annotation for one-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-many relationships
Test friendly — in-memory database for fast, isolated unit and integration tests

rows.description: A Kotlin data class annotated to represent a database table — each property maps to a column
rows.example: @Entity(tableName = "users") data class User(@PrimaryKey val id: Int, val name: String)

rows.description: Data Access Object — an interface containing annotated functions for all database operations
rows.example: @Dao interface UserDao { @Query("SELECT * FROM users") fun getAllUsers(): Flow<List<User>> }

rows.description: Abstract class that extends RoomDatabase — the main entry point for creating and accessing the database
The base class that Room generates — manages the SQLite database connection and provides DAO instances
A class containing @TypeConverter functions that convert unsupported types to/from Room-supported types
A class that defines SQL statements to migrate the database schema from one version to another
rows.example: val MIGRATION_1_2 = object : Migration(1, 2) { override fun migrate(db: SupportSQLiteDatabase) { ... } }

sections.heading: Setting Up Room
sections.content: To use Room, add the room-runtime and room-ktx dependencies to your app-level build.gradle.kts, along with the Room annotation processor (kapt or KSP). KSP (Kotlin Symbol Processing) is the modern and faster alternative to kapt and is now the recommended processor for Room. Once dependencies are added, you define your Entity classes, DAO interfaces, and Database class — Room generates the implementation at compile time.

sections.heading: Room Setup Steps
items: Step 1: Add Room dependencies — implementation("androidx.room:room-runtime:2.6.x") and room-ktx
Step 2: Add KSP processor — ksp("androidx.room:room-compiler:2.6.x") — or kapt if using kapt
Step 3: Apply KSP plugin in build.gradle.kts: id("com.google.devtools.ksp")
Step 4: Create Entity data classes annotated with @Entity
Step 5: Create DAO interfaces annotated with @Dao with your query functions
Step 6: Create the Database abstract class annotated with @Database
Step 7: Build the database instance using Room.databaseBuilder() — typically a singleton
Step 8: Inject the DAO into your Repository using Hilt for dependency injection

rows.description: Marks the class as a Room table; tableName overrides the default (class name lowercase)
Marks a field as the primary key; add autoGenerate = true to auto-increment integer IDs
Auto-generates a unique integer ID for each new row — use with val id: Int = 0 as default
Overrides the column name in the database — useful for matching existing database schemas
Tells Room to ignore this field — it will not be persisted to the database
Flattens a nested object's fields into the parent table's columns — avoids a separate table
Defines a foreign key relationship with another Entity table for referential integrity
Creates a database index on one or more columns to speed up queries on those columns

sections.heading: Data Access Objects (DAOs)
sections.content: The DAO is the heart of Room's query API. It is a Kotlin interface where each function is annotated with a Room query annotation and Room generates the full SQLite implementation at compile time. DAOs support all CRUD operations — Create (Insert), Read (Query), Update, and Delete — as well as complex custom SQL queries. Functions can return plain values, nullable values, suspend functions for coroutines, or Flow for reactive observation.

sections.heading: DAO Query Annotations
items: @Insert — inserts one or more entities; returns Long (row ID) or List<Long> for bulk inserts
@Insert(onConflict = OnConflictStrategy.REPLACE) — replaces existing row on primary key conflict
@Insert(onConflict = OnConflictStrategy.IGNORE) — ignores insert if row with same primary key exists
@Update — updates existing entities matched by primary key; returns Int (number of rows updated)
@Delete — deletes entities matched by primary key; returns Int (number of rows deleted)
@Query("SELECT * FROM users") — executes a custom SQL SELECT query
@Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = :userId") — parameterized query with :paramName syntax
@RawQuery — accepts a SupportSQLiteQuery object for fully dynamic queries built at runtime

rows.description: One-shot insert — returns the row ID of the inserted entity; must be called from a coroutine
One-shot query — returns the result once; nullable if no matching row exists
Reactive query — emits a new list every time the users table changes; no suspend needed
Reactive single entity — emits updated User or null whenever the matching row changes
One-shot update — returns the number of rows updated
One-shot delete — returns the number of rows deleted
Deletes all rows — typically used with @Query("DELETE FROM table_name")
Returns a scalar value — useful for COUNT, SUM, or other aggregate queries

sections.heading: The @Database Class
sections.content: The @Database class is the central access point for the entire Room database. It is an abstract class that extends RoomDatabase and lists all Entity classes in the entities array, along with a version number. The version number is critical — incrementing it without providing a Migration causes Room to destroy and recreate the database by default, losing all data. The @Database class also declares abstract functions that return each DAO interface, which Room implements automatically.

sections.heading: Creating the Database Singleton
items: Use the singleton pattern — only one database instance should exist in the entire app
Create with Room.databaseBuilder(context, AppDatabase::class.java, "app_database.db").build()
Use @Volatile on the INSTANCE field to ensure visibility across threads
Use synchronized(this) {} block with double-checked locking to prevent multiple instantiations
With Hilt: provide the database in a @Module with @Provides @Singleton to ensure a single instance
Use .fallbackToDestructiveMigration() during development to allow schema changes without migrations
Never use fallbackToDestructiveMigration() in production — it deletes all user data on schema change
Use .allowMainThreadQueries() only in tests — never in production code

rows.behavior: Deletes the conflicting row and inserts the new one — effectively an upsert
rows.use_case: Syncing remote data to local — always want the latest version

rows.behavior: Silently ignores the insert if a conflict exists — keeps the original row
rows.use_case: Insert only if not exists — avoid duplicate entries

rows.behavior: Rolls back the current transaction and throws an exception on conflict
rows.use_case: Strict insert — you must handle the conflict explicitly

rows.behavior: Throws an exception but does not roll back previous operations in the transaction
rows.use_case: Rarely used — prefer ABORT for clarity

rows.behavior: Default SQLite behavior — depends on the constraint definition
rows.use_case: Default — let SQLite decide based on the constraint type

sections.heading: TypeConverters — Storing Complex Types
items: Room natively supports: Int, Long, Float, Double, String, Boolean, ByteArray, and their nullable counterparts
For all other types (Date, List, custom objects), you must provide a TypeConverter
Create a class with @TypeConverter functions: one to convert FROM the type TO a supported type, and one to convert back
Example: convert Date to Long (timestamp): @TypeConverter fun dateToLong(date: Date?) = date?.time
Example: convert Long back to Date: @TypeConverter fun longToDate(value: Long?) = value?.let { Date(it) }
Register converters with @TypeConverters(MyConverters::class) on the @Database class
For List<String>, convert to/from JSON String using Gson or Kotlin serialization
Avoid storing large lists as JSON in a single column — normalize into a separate related table instead

rows.description: One parent entity has exactly one child entity
rows.implementation: Use @Embedded or @Relation with a data class holding both entities; query with @Transaction

rows.description: One parent entity has multiple child entities (e.g., User has many Orders)
rows.implementation: Data class with parent and List<Child>; @Relation(parentColumn = "id", entityColumn = "userId")

rows.description: Multiple entities on both sides (e.g., Students and Courses)
rows.implementation: Create a cross-reference @Entity with two foreign keys; use @Relation with associateBy = Junction

rows.description: Required on queries that load relationships — ensures the parent and children are read atomically
rows.implementation: Annotate the DAO function with @Transaction to wrap the multi-table read in a single transaction

sections.heading: Database Migrations
items: Every time you change the database schema (add column, rename table, etc.), increment the version number in @Database

Provide a Migration object: val MIGRATION_1_2 = object : Migration(1, 2) { override fun migrate(db: SupportSQLiteDatabase) { db.execSQL("ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN age INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0") } }
Pass migrations to the builder: Room.databaseBuilder(...).addMigrations(MIGRATION_1_2).build()
Room validates the migration — if the resulting schema does not match the expected schema, it throws an exception
For development only: .fallbackToDestructiveMigration() skips migration and recreates the database
Test migrations using MigrationTestHelper from the room-testing artifact
Auto-migrations (Room 2.4+): @AutoMigration(from = 1, to = 2) in @Database — handles simple schema changes automatically
Complex migrations (column renames, table merges) still require manual Migration objects

rows.description: Creates a persistent database file on disk with the given name
Creates a temporary in-memory database — data lost when process ends; used for testing
Provides Migration objects for schema upgrades between versions
Drops and recreates database when no migration is found — loses all data; development only
Provides a TypeConverter instance when it has dependencies that cannot be auto-created
Specifies the coroutine context for database queries (Room 2.5+)
Permits synchronous queries on the main thread — only for unit tests, never in production
Pre-populates the database from a SQLite file bundled in the assets/ folder

sections.heading: Room with Hilt — Providing Database and DAO
items: In a Hilt @Module, provide the database as a @Singleton: @Provides @Singleton fun provideDatabase(@ApplicationContext context: Context): AppDatabase
Build the database: Room.databaseBuilder(context, AppDatabase::class.java, "app_db").build()
Provide the DAO from the database instance: @Provides fun provideUserDao(db: AppDatabase): UserDao = db.userDao()
DAO does not need @Singleton — it is a thin wrapper and lightweight to create
Inject the DAO into the Repository constructor: class UserRepository @Inject constructor(private val dao: UserDao)
The ViewModel receives the Repository via Hilt — it never directly touches Room or the DAO
Add migration instances to the builder in the Hilt module before calling .build()
Use @ApplicationContext to get the application context — never inject Activity context into a Singleton

rows.mistake: Running Room queries on the main thread
rows.fix: Use suspend functions in DAO and call from viewModelScope, or return Flow which runs on IO automatically

rows.mistake: Incrementing database version without a Migration
rows.fix: Always provide a Migration object or use fallbackToDestructiveMigration() only during development

rows.mistake: Exposing Room entities directly to the ViewModel and UI
rows.fix: Map Room entities to domain model classes in the Repository before exposing to upper layers
Use the singleton pattern or Hilt @Singleton scope — one AppDatabase instance for the app lifetime
Always add @Transaction to DAO functions that query multiple related tables

rows.mistake: Storing large JSON blobs in a single column
rows.fix: Normalize data into separate related tables with proper foreign key relationships
Use MigrationTestHelper and in-memory database in tests to verify migration correctness

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Room is the definitive solution for local database persistence in Android. It wraps SQLite with a type-safe, compile-time-verified, coroutine-friendly API that integrates naturally with the Repository pattern and the broader Jetpack ecosystem. Define your schema with @Entity, declare your operations with @Dao, wire everything together in @Database, and provide a singleton instance via Hilt. Use Flow for reactive database queries that automatically update your UI whenever data changes, and always provide proper Migrations when evolving your schema in production apps.
